lessonPlans.course: United States History and New York State I (Grade 7)
lessonPlans.title: What Was Islip Like Before the Revolution?
lessonPlans.aim: How did the place we call Islip come to be, and why does it have no single founding date?
mainIdeas: The land that became Islip was Native land, home to the Secatogue and other Indigenous people, long before it was a colonial place or a town.

Islip has three different beginnings that mean different things: a 1683 land purchase from Winnaquaheagh to William Nicoll, a 1684 royal patent, and the 1710 act that let the Precinct of Islip elect its own local officials.
A primary source can show us how geography shaped a community, and Nathaniel Conklin's 1798 description tells us about early Islip's farms, bay, road, and settlement pattern.
A community can have a layered origin, a sequence of beginnings, rather than one single founding day.
ssSkills: Read a colonial law and a first-person description as primary sources.
Use maps and land records to explain how geography shaped where people settled.
Compare three kinds of historical beginnings and judge what each one marks.
Support a claim with specific evidence quoted or paraphrased from a source.
Distinguish what a source states from what a reader assumes.
Place Indigenous history at the center of a local origin story rather than treating it as background.
ssPractices: A. Gathering, Interpreting, and Using Evidence

comesAfter: An introduction to what a primary source is and why historians read the actual words people wrote.
A basic look at Indigenous peoples of the Northeast and Long Island before European contact.
The idea that colonial North America developed differently from place to place because of geography, economy, and who held power.
rationale.focus: This lesson opens the unit on early Islip by replacing the simple question 'When was Islip founded?' with a better one: how did this place come to be? Students learn that Islip sits on Secatogue and Indigenous land, and that its government has more than one beginning. They read the 1710 precinct act and Nathaniel Conklin's 1798 description to see how land, water, and law shaped a real community, and they build a layered-origin timeline instead of memorizing a single date.
prepares: The next lesson on the Articles of Association and the choices Islip residents made in 1775 and 1776.
Later lessons that ask who was included in the Revolution's promise of liberty and who was left out.
The habit of reading town records and descriptions carefully, which every later lesson in the unit depends on.

likelyKnow: The Thirteen Colonies existed under British rule before the American Revolution.
Native Americans lived across North America, including Long Island, long before Europeans arrived.
Towns and communities have histories that can be shown on a timeline.
Maps show physical features such as water, roads, and land.
misconceptions: That every town has one clear founding date and one founder.
That the land was empty or unclaimed before European settlers 'started' the town.
That a land purchase and a working local government are the same thing.
That Indigenous people are only a short introduction before the 'real' history begins.
prerequisiteSkills: Reading a short passage of older English and restating it in their own words.
Reading a simple map and naming physical features.
Placing a few dated events in order on a timeline.
masteryChecks: A one-question Do-Now asking students to name one thing that was true about this land before 1683.
A quick thumbs check while modeling the 1710 act: can students point to one job the act let residents do for themselves?
Circulating during the map task to confirm students can locate the bay and the main road.

nysed.title: Native Americans; Colonial Developments
nysed.text: The physical environment and natural resources of North America influenced the development of the first human settlements and the culture of Native Americans (7.1). European exploration and colonization followed; the American colonies developed differently based on economic, social, and geographic factors, and colonial America had a variety of social structures under which not all people were treated equally (7.2).

ccStandards.text: Cite specific textual evidence to support analysis and conclusions.
Determine central ideas or information of a source; provide an accurate summary.
Integrate visual information (maps, charts, images) with print sources.
Write informative/explanatory texts clearly and accurately; select and organize content.

iep.profile: A 12-year-old student with a specific learning disability in reading. This student decodes below grade level, tires quickly with dense older text, and needs extra time to organize written answers, but participates well in discussion and does strong work when directions are broken into short steps.
modifications: Provide the 1710 act and Conklin excerpts in a shortened, modernized version alongside the original wording.
Pre-highlight the two or three sentences the student is responsible for reading closely.
Give a word bank of key terms with plain definitions before reading begins.
Allow the student to underline or annotate rather than write full sentences during source work.
Pair the student with a supportive partner for the map comparison and cooperative source task.
Offer a sentence-starter frame for the exit prompt and the homework paragraph.
Extend time on the written work and accept a shorter response that still names one piece of evidence.
Read directions aloud and check for understanding before independent work.

segments.title: Do-Now + Hook
teacherWillDo: Post the aim on the board: How did the place we call Islip come to be, and why does it have no single founding date?
Give the Do-Now: In one sentence, when and how do you think your own town or neighborhood began?
Ask the hook question aloud: Can a place have more than one beginning?

Take two or three answers, then tell students plainly that Islip has no single founding date, and that its oldest story is a Native one, because this was Secatogue and Indigenous land before it was ever called Islip.
studentsWillDo: Write a one-sentence guess about how their own town or neighborhood began.
Share a guess about whether a place can have more than one beginning.
Listen for the idea that this land has an Indigenous history that comes first.
segments.alignment: Surfaces the misconception of a single founding date and sets up the central idea that Islip's origin is layered and begins with Secatogue land.
scaffoldingQuestions: What is one thing you already know about who lived on Long Island before European settlers?
If you had to pick the year your town 'started,' what would you choose, and why?
higherOrderQuestions: Why might two people disagree about when the same town began?
segments.assessment: Collect the one-sentence Do-Now to check that students can name a beginning and reason about it.

segments.title: Direct Instruction + Guided Organizer: Three Beginnings
segments.transition: Now that we agree a place can have more than one beginning, let's look at three real beginnings for Islip and see what each one actually marks.
teacherWillDo: Build a simple four-part organizer on the board: Native Secatogue land, the 1683 purchase, the 1684 royal patent, and the 1710 precinct act.
Explain that before any deed, this was Secatogue land, and that Winnaquaheagh, a sachem, is named in the 1683 land-transfer story.

Read the 1710 act excerpt aloud and model underlining the actual jobs it let residents do: elect two assessors, a collector, a constable, and a supervisor to assess and collect taxes, keep the peace, and audit their public accounts.
Point out the difference between buying land (1683), getting an official paper from the king's governor with yearly rent owed (1684), and residents governing themselves (1710).
studentsWillDo: Copy the four-part organizer into their notebooks.
Underline, on their own copy of the 1710 act, the jobs residents were now allowed to do for themselves.
Write one sentence naming what each of the three dates marks.
segments.alignment: Teaches students to distinguish three kinds of beginnings and to read a colonial law for the specific powers it granted, directly serving the aim and main ideas.
scaffoldingQuestions: In the 1710 act, name one job the townspeople could now do for themselves.
Who owned this land before the 1683 purchase?
higherOrderQuestions: Which of the three dates, 1683, 1684, or 1710, do you think is the real start of Islip's government, and why?
Why does a land purchase not automatically create a town government?
segments.assessment: Check notebooks for the underlined jobs in the 1710 act and the one-sentence label for each date.

segments.title: Cooperative Source Analysis: Conklin's 1798 Town and the Maps
segments.transition: The 1710 act tells us how Islip governed itself. Now let's use a description and some maps to see how the land itself shaped where people actually lived.
teacherWillDo: Distribute Nathaniel Conklin's 1798 description excerpts: the shape of the town, the bay's bounty, and the who-lived-in-Islip passage.

Set the task: in pairs, list what Conklin says about the town's shape (farms and necks divided by creeks about half a mile apart, houses set along the South Country Road facing the bay, pine plains covering about four fifths of the land) and about the bay's oysters, clams, and waterfowl.
Have pairs open the Maps page and mark where the bay, the road, and the creeks shaped where people lived.

Explain the word 'Mustee' if it comes up in the passage: it is a term of the historical period for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African, quoted only because Conklin used it, and never used as a modern label.
studentsWillDo: In pairs, list three details Conklin gives about the town's geography and resources.
Compare a period map and a modern map on the Maps page and mark the bay, the road, and the creeks.
Write one sentence explaining how the bay, the creeks, and the one main road decided where families built and how they made a living.
segments.alignment: Uses a primary source and maps together so students practice geographic reasoning and see how physical environment shaped settlement, meeting standard 7.1 and the aim.
scaffoldingQuestions: Where did most houses sit, according to Conklin, and which direction did they face?
What did the bay give the people who lived near it?
higherOrderQuestions: Why would families build along one road facing the bay instead of spreading out evenly across the land?
About one in five of the town's roughly 120 households was home to Native, mixed-ancestry, and free Black residents. What does that add to your picture of early Islip?
segments.assessment: Review each pair's list of three geographic details and their marked map to confirm they connected the source to the land.

segments.title: Synthesis + Exit Prompt
segments.transition: Let's pull the three beginnings and Conklin's town together and answer our aim.
teacherWillDo: Rebuild the layered-origin sequence on the board with student help: Secatogue land, the 1683 purchase, the 1684 patent, the 1710 precinct government, and Conklin's 1798 snapshot.
Restate the central idea: Islip has no single founding date, and its oldest story is Indigenous.
Give the exit prompt and collect it as students leave.
studentsWillDo: Help place the beginnings in order on the board.

Write the exit prompt: Name one thing that was true about this land before 1683, and use one detail from either the 1710 act or Conklin's 1798 description to explain why Islip has no single founding date.
segments.alignment: Requires students to answer the aim with evidence and to state why the origin is layered, checking the main ideas of the lesson.
higherOrderQuestions: What is gained, and what is lost, when a town remembers a single founding date instead of its layered history?
segments.assessment: Read exit slips for one pre-1683 fact and one correctly used source detail.

homework.assignment: In one paragraph, answer the aim using evidence: How did the place we call Islip come to be? Name at least one thing that was true before the 1683 purchase, and use one detail from either the 1710 act or Conklin's 1798 description to support your answer. End with a sentence explaining why you think Islip has no single founding date.
requirements: One paragraph of five to eight sentences.
Name at least one thing that was true about this land before 1683 (for example, that it was Secatogue land connected to the sachem Winnaquaheagh).
Use one specific detail from the 1710 act or Conklin's 1798 description as evidence.
End with a sentence explaining why Islip has no single founding date.
answerKey: Before 1683 this was Secatogue and Indigenous land, and Winnaquaheagh, a sachem, is named in the land-transfer story, so the place already had Native history, authority, and meaning.

Strong evidence choices: the 1710 act let residents elect two assessors, a collector, a constable, and a supervisor to assess taxes, keep the peace, and audit their public accounts; or Conklin's 1798 detail that houses stood along the South Country Road facing the bay, with pine plains over about four fifths of the land.

A complete answer explains that 1683 (a purchase), 1684 (a royal patent), and 1710 (self-government) are different kinds of beginnings, so no single date captures the whole story, and the oldest beginning is Indigenous.
Full credit requires one pre-1683 fact, one correctly used source detail, and a closing sentence about the layered origin.

relatedPages.label: Exhibit panel: Before the Town
Person: Winnaquaheagh, Sachem of lands that became Islip
Timeline: William Nicoll's 1683 purchase and Islip Grange
Timeline: Precinct government authorized (1710)
Primary source: Nathaniel Conklin's 1798 Description of the Town of Islip
Maps of the town, past and present

lessonPlans.teacherNote: Keep the Indigenous history at the center, not as an introduction to be moved past. The 1683 deed transferred Secatogue land to William Nicoll, so colonial Islip began on land that already held Native history, authority, and meaning. Be careful with two words. Sachem is the term the sources use for Winnaquaheagh, a Native leader. Mustee, if it comes up in the Conklin passage, is a term of the historical period for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African; explain it as a period word every time it appears, and never let students adopt it as a modern label. Present 1683, 1684, and 1710 as different kinds of beginnings, not competing answers to one trivia question, and tell students plainly that the older story of a single 1683 founding is what this lesson complicates.

lessonPlans.course: United States History and New York State I (Grade 7)
lessonPlans.time: One 42-minute period (about 37 minutes of instruction, with a short buffer)
lessonPlans.title: The Revolution Comes to Islip
lessonPlans.aim: What did it cost ordinary people in a small farming town to take sides in the American Revolution, and how do we know?
mainIdeas: Explain that signing the Islip Precinct Articles of Association on May 10, 1775 was an act of treason against the Crown, and describe the personal risk that choice carried.
Analyze how Isaac Thompson took that risk while holding Crown office, and how in February 1776 Islip residents chose their own militia officers and asked the Provincial Congress to commission them.
Use the 1775 census and the militia figures to picture how small and personal these choices were in a precinct of a few hundred people.
Practice telling what a primary source states from what it does not state, using the April 1776 town gun as a test case.
ssSkills: Read a primary source closely and quote from it to support a claim.
Explain cause and effect across a short span of time, from Lexington and Concord to Islip's local response.
Separate evidence from inference and mark uncertain evidence as uncertain.
Weigh a person's choices against the risks and pressures they faced.
Use numbers from records to reason about scale and about who was left out.
Connect a local decision to a civic idea about taking a public stand.
ssPractices: A. Gathering, Interpreting, and Using Evidence

comesAfter: Lesson 1, in which students learned that Islip has a layered origin on Secatogue and Indigenous land, a 1683 purchase, a 1684 patent, and a 1710 precinct government.
A general Grade 7 unit on the growing tensions with Great Britain, the First Continental Congress, and the outbreak of fighting at Lexington and Concord in April 1775.
rationale.focus: This lesson brings the national story of independence down to the size of one Long Island farming town. Students see that support for the Revolution was a choice real people made in a town meeting, that the choice was legally treason, and that the man who led the town, Isaac Thompson, took the risk while holding Crown office and, at the same time, held enslaved people at Sagtikos Manor. Students also practice a habit of mind they will use all year: reading a source for exactly what it says and refusing to fill the silence with a guess, using the 1776 town gun as the case.
prepares: Lesson 3, on surviving seven years of British occupation, when the same town keeps meeting under the King's required legal dateline.
Later work on how the promise of liberty in 1776 reached some people in Islip and not others.

likelyKnow: The colonies grew angry over taxes and over Parliament's authority, and delegates met as the Continental Congress.
Fighting between colonists and British troops began at Lexington and Concord on April 19, 1775.
A militia was a group of local men, not full-time soldiers, who could be called to serve.
From Lesson 1, that Islip was a small farming precinct near the Great South Bay with its own town meeting.
misconceptions: That everyone in the colonies quickly and firmly agreed to break with Great Britain, when many people were cautious or divided.
That signing a pledge in 1775 was a safe, symbolic act, when it was legally treason and could cost a person their property or life.
That because a document mentions the militia and a gun in the same year, the gun must have been for the militia.
That taking the patriot side meant a person had clean hands, when Isaac Thompson signed a pledge against becoming slaves while holding people in slavery.
prerequisiteSkills: Reading a short primary-source excerpt and restating it in plain words.
Finding a specific quoted phrase inside a passage.
Explaining a simple cause-and-effect link between two events.
masteryChecks: During the Do-Now, listen for whether students can name what happened at Lexington and Concord and roughly when.
In the guided reading, ask a student to point to the exact words in the Articles that show the signers still hoped for peace, checking close-reading skill.
Cold-call one pair to restate the town gun entry in their own words before the sort begins, checking that they can paraphrase a source.

nysed.title: American Independence
nysed.text: Growing tensions over political power and economic issues sparked a movement for independence from Great Britain. New York played a critical role in the course and outcome of the American Revolution.

ccStandards.text: Cite specific textual evidence to support analysis and conclusions.
Determine central ideas or information of a source; provide an accurate summary.
Integrate and compare information from several sources; note discrepancies.
Write arguments to support claims with valid reasoning and relevant, sufficient evidence.

iep.profile: A 12-year-old student with a specific learning disability in reading and a documented processing-speed delay. The student comprehends grade-level ideas when text is chunked and read aloud, but decodes eighteenth-century spelling slowly and loses focus during long independent reading. The student participates well in pairs and when given a clear graphic organizer.
modifications: Provide the Articles of Association and the town gun entry rewritten in a modernized-spelling version printed beside the original, so the student can check meaning against the real words.
Chunk each excerpt into two or three sentences with a one-line prompt after each chunk instead of one long block of text.
Give a partly completed two-column 'states / does not state' organizer with the first row modeled by the teacher.
Pair the student with a supportive partner for the source sort and let the pair produce one shared organizer.
Read all primary-source excerpts aloud, and allow the student to answer the exit prompt by speaking into a device or to the teacher rather than only in writing.
Offer a small word bank for the exit prompt with the terms treason, evidence, and inference defined in plain language.
Extend time on the exit prompt and reduce the required length to two sentences that still show the states-versus-does-not-state distinction.
Check in privately once during independent work to confirm the student has found the key quoted phrase before moving on.

segments.title: Do-Now + Hook
teacherWillDo: Post the Do-Now on the board: 'In April 1775 the men of Islip met, re-elected their supervisor, kept the usual rules about hogs, and settled the town accounts. Write two words for how a town-meeting day like that would feel.'
Give students two minutes to write, then take three quick answers out loud (students often say boring, calm, ordinary).
Deliver the hook: on April 19, 1775, fifteen days after that quiet meeting, fighting broke out at Lexington and Concord in Massachusetts, and the news traveled slowly down the coast to Long Island.
Pose the framing question and hold it: 'If you lived in a small farming town far from the fighting, would you sign a paper taking a public side? What would you want to know first?'
Tell students the aim for the day and post it.
studentsWillDo: Write two words describing an ordinary town-meeting day.
Share a word aloud when called on.
Turn and tell a partner one thing they would want to know before signing a public pledge in 1775.
segments.alignment: The contrast between a routine April meeting and the outbreak of war sets up the aim by making students feel that taking a side was a real, personal decision, not an automatic one.
scaffoldingQuestions: What happened at Lexington and Concord, and about when?
How fast would news travel from Massachusetts to Long Island in 1775?
higherOrderQuestions: Why might people in a quiet farming town hesitate before publicly choosing a side in a fight that started somewhere else?
segments.assessment: Quick read of student two-word responses and the partner shares to confirm they know what Lexington and Concord was and can name a real risk of signing.

segments.title: Direct Instruction + Guided Source Reading
segments.transition: So the war has started far away. Now let us watch what the people of Islip actually did about it.
teacherWillDo: Project the Articles of Association excerpt and read it aloud, then have students underline the phrase 'resolve never to become slaves' and the phrase about a 'reconciliation between Great Britian and America.'
State plainly, in the Town Historian's words, that 'a signature on this paper was an act of treason' against the Crown, and define treason.

Share the numbers on the board: the precinct held about 375 people, about 35 eligible men signed the Islip document, and only 5 were listed as refusing. Note that women, enslaved people, free Black people, Native Americans, children, and Quakers could not sign.

Introduce Isaac Thompson using a two-line biography: he signed the pledge while serving as a Crown magistrate and precinct official, and in April 1776 his neighbors elected him supervisor. Add that he held enslaved people at Sagtikos Manor, so the words 'never to become slaves' sit uneasily against his own household.
Model one line of the 'states / does not state' organizer using the Articles, then hand off to the cooperative activity.
studentsWillDo: Underline the two assigned phrases in the Articles excerpt.
Copy the definition of treason into notes.
Answer a turn-and-talk: why was signing riskier for Isaac Thompson than for many of his neighbors?
Watch the teacher model the organizer and ask one clarifying question if needed.
segments.alignment: This segment builds the first two learning goals directly: students learn that signing was treason, and they meet Thompson's dual role and the contradiction of a slaveholder pledging against slavery.
scaffoldingQuestions: What exactly did the signers promise to do?
Which words show they still hoped to make peace with Great Britain?
What office did Thompson hold that made his signature especially dangerous?
higherOrderQuestions: Why would people in a slaveholding society choose the words 'never to become slaves,' and what is uneasy about that choice given who was working at Sagtikos Manor?
About 35 signed out of a precinct of a few hundred, but many people could not sign at all. How does that change how you read the number?
segments.assessment: Observe underlining and the turn-and-talk to confirm students can identify the treason risk and explain why Thompson's Crown office raised his personal stakes.

segments.title: Cooperative Source Analysis: The Militia and the Town Gun
segments.transition: Signing was one choice. Organizing to fight was another. Let us see how Islip did both in a single winter.
teacherWillDo: Give each pair two short sources: Isaac Thompson's February 9, 1776 letter to the Provincial Congress, and the April 1776 town minute entry on the gun.

Explain the letter in one sentence: Islip residents did not want to be split between the Smithtown and Huntington companies, so they chose their own officers, with Benajah Strong as captain and a company of about 36 or 37 men, and asked the Provincial Congress to grant commissions.
Direct pairs to complete the 'states / does not state' organizer for the town gun entry, 'A Gun and the accutriments paid for at Said Town Meeting which are the property of the towns.'
Circulate and press pairs who write 'the gun was for the militia' to show where the record says that, guiding them to see it does not.
Add a note about Benajah Strong: after occupation began he fled to Connecticut and the British called him a 'notorious rebel,' so choosing to lead the militia carried real cost.
studentsWillDo: Read the two sources in pairs and underline who chose the officers and who was asked to grant the commissions.
Fill in the two-column organizer for the town gun: one column for what the record states, one for what it does not state.
Decide together whether 'the gun was for the militia' belongs in the 'states' column or the 'does not state' column, and be ready to defend the choice.
Add one question they would still need answered before saying what the gun was for.
segments.alignment: This segment carries the militia learning goal and the evidence-versus-inference goal at once: students see Islip organizing itself before the occupation, and they test a tempting inference about the gun against what the record actually says.
scaffoldingQuestions: In the letter, who chose the officers, and who did Islip ask to make it official?
Read the gun sentence slowly. Exactly what did the town do, and what does it never mention?
Which is evidence and which is inference: 'the town bought a gun in 1776' or 'the gun was for the militia'?
higherOrderQuestions: Why would Islip residents want a militia company of their own instead of serving under Smithtown or Huntington officers? What does that say about local identity?
The gun was bought two months after Islip asked for militia commissions. Why is a careful historian still not allowed to say the gun was for the militia?
segments.assessment: Collect or spot-check the pair organizers; a correct response places 'the gun was for the militia' in the 'does not state' column and lists at least one open question.

segments.title: Synthesis + Exit Prompt
segments.transition: Let us pull the choices and the caution together before you leave.
teacherWillDo: Restate the aim and take one sentence from two or three pairs on what a signature cost in 1775.
Remind students that the 1775 census counted 64 men of 375 residents subject to service, and note that this figure is still under review by the project, so it is a working number.
Post the exit prompt and collect it as students leave.
studentsWillDo: Write the exit prompt: 'In one sentence, name one real risk a person in Islip took by signing the Articles of Association. In a second sentence, state one thing the town gun record does NOT tell us.'
Hand the exit slip to the teacher on the way out.
segments.alignment: The exit prompt checks both halves of the aim in two sentences: the personal cost of taking a side, and the discipline of naming what a source does not say.
higherOrderQuestions: If you had lived in Islip in 1775, what one fact would have most affected your decision to sign or not to sign?
segments.assessment: Exit slips are scored for two things: a specific, accurate risk of signing (treason, loss of property or life) and a correct statement of a gap in the town gun record (it does not say the purpose).

homework.assignment: Write two short paragraphs about the April 1776 town gun. The minutes record only that the town paid for 'a Gun and the accutriments' and that it was 'the property of the towns.' In the first paragraph, state only what the record actually tells us. In the second, list the questions you would still need answered before you could say what the gun was for, and explain why a careful historian would not simply assume it was for the militia.
requirements: Paragraph one uses only facts the record states, with no guesses.
Paragraph two lists at least two specific questions that remain open.
The response ends by explaining, in one sentence, why the 1776 date alone does not prove the gun's purpose.
Quote the phrase 'the property of the towns' at least once and use it correctly.
answerKey: Paragraph one should say only that at the April 1776 town meeting the town paid for a gun and its equipment and recorded that the gun was town property, owned by the whole town rather than by one person.
It should not claim the gun was for the militia, for defense, or for any stated purpose, because the minutes give none.

Paragraph two might ask: who used or stored the gun, why the town spent public money on it, whether other records mention it, and whether it connects to the militia company organized two months earlier.
The closing sentence should explain that a suggestive date is not the same as a stated purpose, and that connecting the gun to the militia would be an inference the record does not support.

lessonPlans.teacherNote: Two cautions matter most here. First, the town gun: the April 1776 minutes record only that the town bought 'a Gun and the accutriments' as public property. They do not state its purpose, and this project does not claim it was for the militia. Present the 1776 date as suggestive and the purpose as an open research question, and use it to model honest reading of a source. Second, the 1775 census figure of 64 men subject to service is still under review by the project, so present it as a working figure rather than a settled fact. When students read that the signers vowed 'never to become slaves,' remind them that Isaac Thompson himself held enslaved people at Sagtikos Manor, an average of about four in the decades around 1790, 1800, and 1810; the contradiction is part of the honest story, not a detail to smooth over. Keep the King's-name issue out of this lesson; it belongs to Lesson 3, where the wartime dateline is the required legal formula.

lessonPlans.course: United States History and New York State I (Grade 7)
lessonPlans.title: Living Under Occupation
lessonPlans.aim: How did ordinary families in Islip survive seven years of British occupation, and why can the words in an official record fail to tell us what people really believed?
mainIdeas: Describe what daily life was like for civilians on occupied Long Island, including quartered soldiers, seized property, and whaleboat raids from across Long Island Sound.
Explain that dating the town minutes by the reign of King George III was a required legal formula for records made under Crown authority, not evidence that Islip's people were Loyalists.

Analyze how Islip families were caught between British occupiers, New York State authority, Connecticut raiders, and wartime suspicion, so the story is more complicated than patriots against Loyalists.
Read an official record carefully and decide what its wording does and does not prove, marking uncertain evidence as uncertain.
ssSkills: Read a short primary source and pull out concrete details about daily life
Separate what a record proves from what it does not prove
Place occupation events in order from 1776 to 1783
Compare two or more sources and notice where they disagree
Explain a cause-and-effect chain, such as raids leading to property loss and fear
Support a claim with evidence quoted from the sources
ssPractices: A. Gathering, Interpreting, and Using Evidence

comesAfter: Lesson 1, where students learned how Islip's town government worked at annual April meetings
Lesson 2, where students studied the 1775 Articles of Association and how residents were asked to choose sides before the war reached Long Island
rationale.focus: This lesson moves from the choice to take sides into the long occupation that followed. Students look at what happened after the British took Long Island in 1776: soldiers in homes, property taken, whaleboat raids on the shore, and a town that kept caring for its poor the whole time. The heart of the lesson is a reading skill. Students learn that an official record can carry the King's name in its heading because the law required it, even while the same town kept re-electing a man who had signed a patriot pledge.
prepares: Lesson 4, on how New York State authority replaced Crown authority after 1783 and how the town's records changed
Later work on reading any historical document for what its wording can and cannot prove

likelyKnow: The American Revolution was a war between the American colonies and Great Britain
The war reached New York, and the British controlled New York City and Long Island for much of it
Islip held yearly town meetings and elected officers such as a supervisor and overseers of the poor
In 1775 many Islip residents signed the Articles of Association to support the patriot cause
misconceptions: Believing that everyone on occupied Long Island was either a firm patriot or a firm Loyalist, with no one caught in between
Thinking that if a town record is dated by the King's reign, the townspeople must have supported the King
Assuming raids on Islip came only from the British, when many came from Connecticut whaleboat crews
Expecting a town's own records to describe wartime hardship in detail, when Islip's minutes stay quiet about it
prerequisiteSkills: Reading a short excerpt of eighteenth-century English aloud and restating it in plain words
Finding one specific detail in a source to back up an answer
Working in a small group and reporting out to the class
masteryChecks: During the Do-Now, listen to how students restate the King's-name heading in their own words
Check the group organizer to confirm each group found at least one concrete detail from its source
Use the exit prompt to confirm students can name one thing a record proves and one thing it does not

nysed.title: American Independence
nysed.text: Growing tensions over political power and economic issues sparked a movement for independence from Great Britain. New York played a critical role in the course and outcome of the American Revolution.

ccStandards.text: Cite specific textual evidence to support analysis and conclusions.
Compare the point of view of two or more authors; assess how point of view shapes content.
Integrate and compare information from several sources; note discrepancies.
Draw evidence from informational texts to support analysis, reflection, and research.

iep.profile: A 12-year-old student with a specific learning disability in reading who decodes slowly and struggles to hold onto long eighteenth-century sentences. The student follows discussion well and has strong ideas out loud, but tires quickly when asked to read dense text alone.
modifications: Provide each source excerpt in a shortened, modernized version alongside the original so the student can check the plain-language version
Pre-teach the key terms occupation, quartered, plunder, and whaleboat with a picture or a one-line meaning before the lesson
Pair the student with a reading partner during group work so text is read aloud, not silently
Give a partly filled graphic organizer with the source titles already written in, so the student adds only the details
Allow the student to answer the exit prompt by talking to the teacher or recording a short voice note instead of writing
Highlight the two or three sentences in each source that carry the details the student needs, to reduce the amount of text to search
Extend time on the homework and accept a shorter journal entry of five to six sentences that still uses two source details

keyTerms: plunder (to rob or take property by force)
Whaleboat War (raids by small boats crossing Long Island Sound)
poor relief (the town's duty to support its poorest residents)
legal formula (required wording, such as dating a record by the King's reign)
Loyalist (a colonist who stayed loyal to Great Britain)
apparently (a word historians use when evidence is likely but not certain)

segments.title: Do-Now + Hook
teacherWillDo: Before class, write this line on the board: "At a Town Meeting of Said Presinct held the first Tuesday of April in the Seventeenth year of his present Majesties Reign George the Third King of Great Britain."
Ask students to read it quietly and jot two answers: Who does this record seem to belong to, and does it sound like it was written by people who supported the King or opposed him?
Collect three or four guesses without saying which is right, and post them so the class can return to them at the end.
studentsWillDo: Read the heading and write two quick answers in their notebooks
Share a guess about whether the writers supported or opposed the King
Notice that the same heading names the King but comes from an Islip town meeting
segments.transition: We open with the exact words at the top of a real Islip town record from the middle of the war.
segments.alignment: Sets up the central aim by making students form a first guess about what the King's-name heading proves, a guess they will test against evidence.
scaffoldingQuestions: Whose name is in the heading?
Where and when was this meeting held?
What is your first guess about the people who wrote it?
higherOrderQuestions: Can the words a record is required to use tell us what the writers actually believed?
segments.assessment: Listen to how students restate the heading to check that they can read it and form a claim about it.

segments.title: Direct Instruction + Guided Organizer
teacherWillDo: Give a short mini-lecture on the occupation: the British took Long Island in 1776 and held it until 1783, so Islip families lived seven years under military control.
Name the pressures with quick examples from the essays: soldiers quartered in homes, fences and woodlots burned for firewood, and livestock "purchased," seized, or stolen.
Explain the Whaleboat War in plain terms: because everyone on the island counted as a British subject, raiders in small boats crossed the Sound from Connecticut and plundered Long Island shores.
Introduce a class chart with two columns, "What the record proves" and "What the record does not prove," and start it with the King's-name heading.
studentsWillDo: Take notes on a guided organizer with three boxes: soldiers, property, and raids
Write one example under each box from the mini-lecture
Help start the class chart by suggesting what the King's-name heading does and does not prove
segments.transition: Before we read the sources, we need a picture of what occupation actually did to daily life.
segments.alignment: Builds the background knowledge students need to interpret the sources and models the proves versus does-not-prove reasoning at the center of the lesson.
scaffoldingQuestions: What are three things soldiers might take or use on a farm?
Who was doing the whaleboat raids, and where did they come from?
Why did being a British subject leave Long Island open to plunder?
higherOrderQuestions: If a town clerk is required by law to date a record by the King's reign, what does that dating prove and what does it not prove?
segments.assessment: Scan organizers to confirm each student recorded at least one example under each of the three boxes.

segments.title: Cooperative Source Analysis
teacherWillDo: Split the class into three groups and give each one source cluster: the wartime town minutes under the King's name (town-minutes-wartime), the raid and plundering records (onderdonk-raids and plundering-resolution-1781), and the poor-relief entries on Johanna Hutton (town-minutes-poor-relief).
Give every group the same two questions: what does daily life look like in your source, and what pressure were Islip families caught between?
Circulate and prompt groups to quote one exact detail as evidence.
Point out that the 1781 state resolution said Long Island residents should not automatically be treated as British subjects even while Connecticut boats plundered them.
studentsWillDo: Read their assigned source cluster with a partner
Write answers to the two shared questions, including one quoted detail
Prepare to teach their source to the rest of the class in two or three sentences
segments.transition: Now you become the readers, and each group takes one piece of the story.
segments.alignment: Gives students direct practice gathering evidence from primary sources and comparing what different records reveal about occupied life.
scaffoldingQuestions: What is one thing your source says was taken, feared, or done?
Who is putting pressure on Islip families in your source?
What exact words will you quote as your evidence?
higherOrderQuestions: Name the different forces Islip families were caught between. How does that change the simple patriots-versus-Loyalists story?
segments.assessment: Collect each group's written answers and check that every group cited one exact detail from its source.

segments.title: Share-Out and Reading the Record's Wording
teacherWillDo: Have each group teach its source in two or three sentences while the class adds details to the shared proves versus does-not-prove chart.

Test the King's-name heading against the fact that the same wartime meetings kept re-electing Isaac Thompson, who had signed the patriot Articles of Association, so the heading proves the record was made under Crown authority but does not prove the townspeople were Loyalists.
Point to the Johanna Hutton entries, where nearby accounts pay "Joanna Hudson," and explain why historians say the two names are apparently the same woman rather than stating it as fact.
Guide the class to the big idea: an official record's wording can be required, so we must ask what it proves before we trust it.
studentsWillDo: Teach their source to the class and add to the shared chart
Explain why the King's-name heading does not prove loyalty when the same meetings re-elected a patriot signer
Explain why "apparently" is the careful word for the Hutton and Hudson spellings
segments.transition: Let's put the three sources together and finally test our opening guess.
segments.alignment: Brings the sources together to answer the aim and makes the reading-a-record skill explicit through two real examples of careful wording.
scaffoldingQuestions: What does the King's-name heading prove for certain?
What does it not prove about what people believed?
Why do we say the town supported someone named apparently Hudson or Hutton?
higherOrderQuestions: If the same meeting can carry the King's name and re-elect a patriot, what does that tell you about reading only the heading of any document?
segments.assessment: The completed class chart shows whether students can separate what the wartime record proves from what it does not.

homework.assignment: Imagine you are part of a farming family in Islip during the occupation. Using at least two details from the sources you read today, write a short journal entry, about one page, describing one week of your life. Include one thing you fear, one ordinary task that still must be done, and one careful decision your family has to make. Do not invent famous events. Keep your entry grounded in what the records actually show about occupied Islip.
requirements: About one page, or five to six sentences for students using the modified length
Uses at least two specific details drawn from the day's sources
Names one fear, one ordinary task, and one careful family decision
Stays realistic and does not add famous people or invented battles
answerKey: A strong entry names a real occupation pressure, such as soldiers quartered in the home, fences or woodlots burned for firewood, or livestock taken.
A strong entry references a raid, such as the whaleboat crews robbing William Nicoll's farm or the boats attacking a sloop in the South Bay.
The ordinary task should be believable farm or town work, such as caring for animals, saving grain, or paying a share toward poor relief.
The careful decision should reflect being caught between forces, such as how to answer soldiers, whether to hide provisions, or how to stay safe during a raid.
The entry should stay grounded in the records and avoid claiming spying, secret meetings, or events the sources do not show.

lessonPlans.teacherNote: Two cautions anchor this lesson. First, the King's-name dating in the wartime minutes was the standard legal formula that clerks were required to use for records made under Crown authority in occupied territory. It is not proof that the townspeople were Loyalists or supported the King. Make this an explicit teaching point: the same meetings that carried that heading kept re-electing Isaac Thompson, who had signed the patriot Articles of Association. Second, "Johanna Hutton" and "Joanna Hudson" in the 1782 and 1783 records are apparently the same woman with her name spelled two ways, so present that identification as apparently, not as certain fact. Resist flattening the occupation into a simple patriot-versus-Loyalist story, since the evidence shows people caught between occupiers, Connecticut raiders, state authority, and wartime suspicion all at once.

lessonPlans.course: United States History and New York State I (Grade 7)
lessonPlans.title: Who Belonged in Early Islip?
lessonPlans.aim: The Revolution promised liberty in 1776. Who in early Islip shared in that promise, and who was left out?
mainIdeas: Explain that the Revolution spoke the language of liberty, but that liberty was not yet shared by everyone who lived in Islip.
Identify Native, mixed-ancestry, free Black, and enslaved people as part of Islip's real early population, not as a background to it.
Read a short primary source closely and describe what it does and does not tell us about the people it names.
Weigh what it means that many of these residents appear in the records only briefly, often without full names, and explain why historians must handle those records with care.
ssSkills: Reading a primary source closely and separating what it states from what it does not state
Using a period term, such as 'Mustee,' accurately and only as a historical word, never as a modern label
Comparing more than one kind of record about the same community
Turning a count of houses into a fraction and explaining what that fraction shows
Recognizing silences and gaps in a record as evidence to be interpreted with care
Writing a short evidence-based claim that names at least one real person or group
ssPractices: A. Gathering, Interpreting, and Using Evidence

comesAfter: Lesson 2, in which students learned that signing the 1775 Articles of Association was an act of treason and studied who chose to sign.
Lesson 3, in which students practiced deciding what a record does and does not prove during the occupation.
rationale.focus: This lesson turns from who took political risks to who was never given the chance to take them. Students use three short Islip records to test the word 'all' in the promise of liberty against the town's real population: Conklin's 1798 count of households, the 1783 marriage record of York and Elizabeth, and a teacher summary of enslaved and indentured labor at Sagtikos Manor.
prepares: Lesson 5, which traces how belonging in Islip kept widening in the generations after independence.
Later study of slavery, emancipation, and citizenship in New York State and the early republic.

likelyKnow: The Declaration of Independence used the words 'all men are created equal' and spoke of liberty.
Slavery existed in the American colonies and in New York, including on Long Island, and was never confined to the South.
From earlier lessons, that signing the Articles of Association in 1775 was risky and open only to certain residents.
misconceptions: That the promise of liberty in 1776 reached everyone who lived in a town.
That slavery was a Southern institution and did not exist in a small Long Island town like Islip.
That early Islip was a town of only white settler families.
That a person is unimportant to history if the records say little about them.
prerequisiteSkills: Reading a short quoted primary source and putting it in their own words.
Turning a simple count, such as 25 out of about 120, into a rough fraction.
Telling apart what a source states from what a reader adds or guesses.
masteryChecks: The Do-Now asks students to test the word 'all,' which surfaces the misconception that liberty reached everyone.
A quick cold-call after the first record checks that students can restate Conklin's count as roughly one household in five.
Circulating during the two-column note sheet confirms students can keep 'what the record tells us' separate from 'what it does not tell us.'

nysed.title: Colonial Developments
nysed.text: The American colonies were established for a variety of reasons and developed differently based on economic, social, and geographic factors. Colonial America had a variety of social structures under which not all people were treated equally. Over the course of the 17th and 18th centuries, slavery grew in the colonies (7.2e).

ccStandards.text: Cite specific textual evidence to support analysis and conclusions.
Determine central ideas or information of a source; provide an accurate summary.
Integrate and compare information from several sources; note discrepancies.
Write arguments to support claims with valid reasoning and relevant, sufficient evidence.

iep.profile: A 12-year-old student with a specific learning disability in reading who decodes about two grade levels below peers and tires quickly with dense text. This student understands ideas well when they are read aloud or shortened, and participates readily in paired talk but hesitates to write full paragraphs without a frame.
modifications: Provide each of the three records on its own half-page card in a large, clean font, with the key sentence highlighted.
Read the Conklin sentence, the York and Elizabeth record, and the Sagtikos summary aloud before students work with them.
Preteach the terms 'Mustee,' 'servant,' 'enslaved,' and 'free' with a short visual word bank the student keeps at hand.
Shrink the two-column note sheet to two rows per record and let the student use short phrases or bullet points instead of sentences.
Pair the student with a supportive partner for reading and note-taking, keeping written output shared.
Offer a sentence starter for the exit prompt, such as 'One person the records prove lived in Islip is ___, and one thing we still do not know is ___.'
Allow the writing prompt to be completed in two to three sentences, or dictated, rather than a full paragraph.
Check in privately once during independent work to confirm the student is separating what the record says from what it does not say.

keyTerms: Mustee (a period term for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African, explained every time and never used as a modern label)

segments.title: Do-Now + Hook
teacherWillDo: Write on the board: 'All men are created equal.' Ask students, as a Do-Now, to answer in writing: What does the word 'all' seem to promise?
After two minutes, add a second question and give one more minute: In 1776, did that promise actually reach everyone who lived in a town like Islip?

Take three or four answers out loud without judging them, then tell students that today they will hold the word 'all' up against real Islip records and count who was inside the promise and who was left out.
studentsWillDo: Write a quick response to the Do-Now about what 'all' promises.
Predict, in a sentence, whether the promise reached everyone in a town like Islip.
Share a prediction aloud when called on.
segments.alignment: Surfaces the misconception that liberty in 1776 reached everyone and sets up the aim, giving students a claim they will test against evidence.
scaffoldingQuestions: What people do you picture when you read the word 'all' in that sentence?
Who lives in a town besides the men who owned land and could vote?
higherOrderQuestions: Can a promise be real and still be unfinished at the same time?
segments.assessment: Quick read of Do-Now responses and predictions to gauge starting assumptions.

segments.title: Direct Instruction + Guided Reading of Conklin's 1798 Count
segments.transition: To test the word 'all,' we start with a number: how many Islip households in 1798 were home to Native, mixed-ancestry, and free Black families.
teacherWillDo: Project Supervisor Nathaniel Conklin's 1798 sentence: 'There are in this Town about one hundred and twenty dweling houses twenty-five of which Indians, Mustees and free Negros live in.'

Explain that 'Mustee' is a term from the seventeen and eighteen hundreds for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African, and that the class uses it only because it is the word the record uses, never as a label for people today.
Model turning the count into a fraction on the board: 25 of about 120 houses is roughly one household in five.
Introduce the two-column note sheet, 'What the record tells us' and 'What the record does not tell us,' and fill in the first row together for the Conklin sentence.
studentsWillDo: Read the Conklin sentence and restate it in their own words.
Work out with the teacher that about one in five Islip households was Native, mixed-ancestry, or free Black.
Complete the first row of the two-column sheet, noting that the record proves these households existed but does not give the people's names or life stories.
segments.alignment: Gives students the first piece of evidence that early Islip was never a town of only white settler families, directly addressing the aim and the NYSED standard on unequal social structures.
scaffoldingQuestions: About how many houses did Conklin count in the whole town?
What is 25 out of 120, roughly, as a fraction?
Does the sentence give us any names?
higherOrderQuestions: Conklin groups three different communities into one sentence. What does that grouping tell us, and what does it hide about these neighbors as distinct people?
Conklin wrote this to win a boundary argument with Huntington, not to record the town's people. How might that purpose have shaped what he counted?
segments.assessment: Cold-call to confirm students can state the count as roughly one household in five, and a check of the first note-sheet row.

segments.title: Cooperative Source Analysis: York, Elizabeth, and Sagtikos
segments.transition: The Conklin sentence counts households but names almost no one. Two more records let us come closer to real people.
teacherWillDo: Give each pair the 1783 St. John's Episcopal Church record: 'York, a Black servant to William Nicoll, Esq.' married 'Elizabeth, a free Indian Woman,' and a short summary of labor at Sagtikos Manor.

Explain the Sagtikos summary: most of the farm and household work there was done by enslaved and indentured people, and New York State census records show that Isaac Thompson held on average about four enslaved people in the decades around 1790, 1800, and 1810, whose fuller stories are still being researched.
Ask each pair to complete the two remaining rows of the note sheet, one for York and Elizabeth and one for Sagtikos, keeping 'tells us' and 'does not tell us' separate.
Circulate to make sure pairs treat 'servant' with care, since in this period the word could describe an enslaved person.
studentsWillDo: Read the marriage record and the Sagtikos summary with a partner.

Fill in the two-column sheet for both, noting, for example, that the record proves York and Elizabeth married in 1783 and that York is called a servant while Elizabeth is called free, but does not tell us York's own status for certain, their ages, or how they came to Islip.
Mark on the sheet where the Sagtikos summary says the fuller story is still being researched.
segments.alignment: Moves from a group count to two named individuals and to enslaved laborers, building the comparison across sources that the aim requires and letting students weigh how little the record preserves.
scaffoldingQuestions: How is York described? How is Elizabeth described?
What does the word 'free' next to Elizabeth's name tell us that is not said about York?
Who did most of the work at Sagtikos Manor?
higherOrderQuestions: York and Elizabeth appear in a single marriage line. Why might a Black servant and a free Indian woman leave such a small trace when landowning families fill whole pages?
The Articles of Association pledged that the signers would 'never become slaves,' yet enslaved people were working at Sagtikos Manor. How do you hold both of those facts at the same time?
segments.assessment: Review of the completed two-column note sheet, checking that students separate stated fact from inference across all three records.

segments.title: Whole-Class Share + Exit Prompt
segments.transition: With all three records in front of us, we can answer the question we opened with.
teacherWillDo: Lead a brief share-out, building a class list on chart paper of the real people and groups the records prove lived in Islip: the households Conklin counted, York, Elizabeth, and the enslaved people at Sagtikos Manor.

Return to the Do-Now question and ask students, in the exit prompt, to answer on a half-sheet: name one person or group the records prove shared Islip but was left outside the promise of liberty, and one question they still have about someone the records did not fully name.
Collect the exit slips as a check for understanding.
studentsWillDo: Add to the class list of proven residents.
Write the exit prompt response naming a real person or group and one open question.
Turn in the exit slip.
segments.alignment: Requires each student to answer the aim in writing with named evidence and to leave a genuine research question open, modeling honest history.
scaffoldingQuestions: Which of today's three records named an actual person?
What is one thing the records could not tell you?
higherOrderQuestions: If these residents are missing from most of the record, what is the responsibility of people studying Islip's history now?
segments.assessment: Exit slip graded for a named real person or group and a genuine open question.

homework.assignment: In one paragraph, answer the aim using at least two of today's records: who shared in the promise of liberty in early Islip, and who did not? Then design a short memorial marker or museum label, three to four sentences, for York and Elizabeth or for the twenty-five households Conklin counted, writing honestly about what is known and what is not.
requirements: Use at least two of the three records from class (Conklin's 1798 count, the York and Elizabeth marriage record, the Sagtikos summary).
Name at least one real person or group whose presence the records prove.
Include at least one sentence that states plainly something the records do not tell us.
Invent no details the records do not give.
Use the word 'Mustee' only as a period term, if at all, and explain it.
answerKey: A strong response explains that liberty in 1776 was promised in the language of the Revolution but was not shared by enslaved people, free Black people, or Native people, who could not sign the Articles of Association.

It names real presence from the records: about one in five Islip households in 1798 was Native, mixed-ancestry, or free Black; York, a Black servant to William Nicoll, and Elizabeth, a free Indian woman, married in 1783; and enslaved and indentured people did much of the labor at Sagtikos Manor, where Isaac Thompson held on average about four enslaved people in the decades around 1790, 1800, and 1810.

The marker or label states what is known while marking what is not, for example that the records prove these people lived and worked in Islip but rarely give full names or life stories, and that the fuller history is still being researched.
The response avoids inventing biography and uses 'Mustee' only as an explained period term.
