lessonPlans.teacherNote: One caution controls this lesson. The wartime town minutes from 1777 to 1783 open each meeting by the reign of King George III. That dating was the required legal formula for official records made under Crown authority during the occupation, not evidence that Islip's people were Loyalists. Say so plainly when it comes up: the same town re-elected Isaac Thompson, who had signed the patriot Articles of Association in 1775, at every one of those meetings, and in April 1784 the clerk opened the record by the authority of the people of the State of New York instead. Keep the point civic: what stayed constant through peace, war, and re-founding was the town governing itself. Note also that participation in the historical town meeting was limited to freeholders and inhabitants who qualified, so women, enslaved people, free Black residents, and Native residents could not vote or hold these offices; use that honestly as a bridge to how the franchise has widened, not as a reason to treat the meeting as less real.

sourceActivities.title: Signing the Articles of Association, 1775

source.label: The Islip Precinct Articles of Association (Primary Sources)

sourceActivities.excerpt: We, the Freemen, Freeholders, and inhabitants of Islip Precinct... do, in the most solemn manner, resolve never to become slaves; and do associate under the ties of religion, honor, and love to our country to adopt and endeavor to carry into execution, whatever measure may be recommended by the Continental Congress.
sourceActivities.excerptAttribution: The Islip Precinct Articles of Association, May 10, 1775
sourceActivities.context: On May 10, 1775, weeks after the fighting at Lexington and Concord, Islip Precinct held a special meeting where residents could sign a local pledge to support the decisions of the Continental Congress. Signing was a serious act. The Town Historian explains that putting your name on this paper was treated as treason against the King. The record notes that about 35 eligible men signed, and only five residents were listed as refusing.

vocabulary.definition: A written pledge that residents signed to promise they would support the Continental Congress and stand together against new British taxes and laws.
A person who owned land. In this period, land ownership often decided who could vote and sign public documents.
The crime of acting against your own government or ruler. Signing this pledge could be treated as treason against the King.
Making peace and settling a disagreement. The signers said they still hoped Great Britain and America could reconcile.

sourcingQuestions: Who wrote and signed this document, and what were they trying to do by putting their names on it?
When was this signed, and what recent event (Lexington and Concord) might have pushed Islip residents to act in May 1775?
This text comes to us through the Town Historian's records. Why might a historian want to know exactly how many people signed and how many refused?
closeReadingQuestions: The signers say they "resolve never to become slaves." What do you think they meant by that phrase, and who in Islip was actually held in slavery at the time and could not sign?
Find the words that show the signers still hoped for peace with Great Britain. Does the document sound like people who wanted a total break, or something more cautious?

The record says women, enslaved people, free Black people, Native Americans, children, and Quakers were excluded or could not sign. How does that change how you read the words "We, the Freemen, Freeholders, and inhabitants of Islip Precinct"?
historicalThinkingQuestions: Only about 35 people signed, out of a precinct of roughly 375 residents. What does that number tell us, and what does it leave out?
Signing was risky and could be called treason. Why might a person choose to sign anyway, and why might a neighbor choose not to?

This document shows Islip residents making a political choice before any British soldiers arrived. How does that complicate the idea that people on Long Island were simply forced into one side or the other?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: When people today sign a petition or add their name to a public statement, what are they risking or hoping to gain? How is that similar to or different from signing this pledge in 1775?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: Imagine you are a resident of Islip Precinct in May 1775 deciding whether to sign. Write a short paragraph explaining your choice. Name one reason to sign and one reason to be afraid, and be clear about which choice you make.

sourceActivities.title: The 1776 Town Gun: Evidence and Inference

source.label: Town Offices on the Eve of Revolution, and a Town Gun (Primary Sources)

sourceActivities.excerpt: A Gun and the accutriments paid for at Said Town Meeting which are the property of the towns.
sourceActivities.context: At the April 1776 town meeting, the clerk recorded that the town paid for "a Gun and the accutriments" and that these were the property of the town. That same year, elected leaders Isaac Thompson and Benajah Strong were helping organize Islip's militia company. It is tempting to connect the gun to the militia, but the minutes do not say why the town bought it. The date is suggestive, and the purpose is still an open research question.

vocabulary.definition: The extra equipment that goes with a piece of gear. For a gun, this could mean things like a powder horn, shot, or a carrying strap. The clerk spelled it "accutriments."
Something owned by the whole town together, not by one person. The minutes say the gun belonged to the town.
The facts a source actually gives you. Here, the evidence is that the town bought a gun and recorded it as town property in April 1776.
A guess or conclusion you reach by reasoning beyond the evidence. Saying the gun was "for the militia" is an inference, not something the record states.

sourcingQuestions: What kind of record is this, and why would a town clerk write down that the town had bought a gun?
The minutes list who was elected to town offices in the same year. How does knowing that Thompson and Strong were both town officers and militia organizers shape the questions we can ask?
This entry is very short. What does the clerk choose to record, and what does the clerk leave out?
closeReadingQuestions: Read the sentence carefully. Exactly what does it tell you the town did, and what does it never mention?
The minutes call the gun "the property of the towns." Why might it matter that the gun belonged to the whole town rather than to one person?
Which of these statements is evidence and which is inference: (a) "The town bought a gun in 1776," and (b) "The gun was for the militia"? Explain how you can tell them apart.
historicalThinkingQuestions: Why is it important for a historian to say clearly which parts of a claim come from the source and which parts are a guess?
What other reasons, besides a militia, might a town in 1776 have had to own a gun? List a few possibilities you cannot rule out.
If you could find one more document to help answer why the town bought the gun, what would you look for, and where might you search?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Today we often see a fact online and quickly assume what it means. How can practicing the difference between evidence and inference help you judge what you read now?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: Write two short sentences about the town gun. In the first, state only what the record proves. In the second, state a careful guess about its purpose and begin it with the words "We cannot be sure, but it is possible that..."

sourceActivities.title: Meeting Under the King's Name, 1777 to 1783

source.label: Wartime Town Meetings Under the King's Name (Primary Sources)

sourceActivities.excerpt: At a Town Meeting of Said Presinct held the first Tuesday of April in the Seventeenth year of his present Majesties Reign George the Third King of Great Britain &:C:... Isaac Thompson Supervisor.
sourceActivities.context: British and Loyalist forces occupied Long Island from September 1776 until late 1783. Through all of it, Islip held its yearly town meeting every April, elected its usual officers, and kept its accounts. Because these were legal records made under Crown authority during the occupation, the clerk was required to date each meeting by the reign of King George III. This was the standard legal dating formula clerks had to use. It is not evidence that the townspeople were Loyalists or supported the King. Isaac Thompson, who had signed the patriot Articles of Association in 1775, was re-elected supervisor at every one of these meetings.

vocabulary.definition: When a foreign army takes control of a place and stays there. British and Loyalist forces occupied Long Island for most of the war.
Counting years by how long a king has reigned. "The Seventeenth year of his present Majesties Reign" means the 17th year that George III had been king.
The official, required wording clerks had to use to date a legal record. Under Crown rule, that formula named the king, whatever the clerk's own beliefs.
A colonist who stayed loyal to the King and Great Britain during the Revolution. The King's name in these records does not by itself make the townspeople Loyalists.

sourcingQuestions: Who wrote these headings, and why was a town clerk required to use this exact wording during the occupation?
These meetings happened while a foreign army controlled Long Island. How might occupation have affected what the clerk was allowed to write?
The same man, Isaac Thompson, signed a patriot pledge in 1775 and is named supervisor in these records. Why is it useful to know that about the person in the source?
closeReadingQuestions: The 1777 heading dates the meeting by "his present Majesties Reign George the Third." Based on the context, is that the clerk's personal opinion, or a required legal formula? How can you tell?
Aside from the dateline, what ordinary town business is still happening in these meetings? What does that tell you about daily life under occupation?
Why might it be a mistake to read the words "King George the third" and conclude that Islip's people supported the King?
historicalThinkingQuestions: Historians warn against reading the King's name here as proof of loyalty. Why is it important to understand the rules a source had to follow before judging what it means?
How can a town "keep governing itself" and re-elect a patriot supervisor while its records still carry the King's name? What does that reveal about how messy the road to independence really was?
Compare these wartime headings with the way you would expect a town to record a meeting. What surprises you, and what does that surprise teach you about occupied Long Island?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Sometimes people must follow official rules or formats they did not choose and may not agree with. How can knowing the rules behind a document help you understand what it really tells you about the people who made it?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: A classmate reads "King George the third" in these minutes and says, "So Islip supported the King." Write a short reply, three or four sentences, explaining why the King's name in the dateline does not prove that.

sourceActivities.title: Caring for Johanna Hutton, 1782 to 1783

source.label: Caring for the Poor in the Town Minutes (Primary Sources)

sourceActivities.excerpt: It is Voted by the majority of the town that Johanah Hutton & Her Child for to begin at the west End of the town[.] & Each family to ceape [keep] ther in perposhion [proportion] as they are Rated.
sourceActivities.context: Long before public welfare systems, the town itself was responsible for its poorest residents, and overseers of the poor were elected every April. In 1783, in the middle of the occupation, the town voted to care for Johanna Hutton and her child by having families take turns keeping them, family by family, in proportion to what each was taxed. Account entries written beside the 1782 minutes record payments to a "Joanna Hudson," apparently the same woman with her name spelled a different way.

vocabulary.definition: Town officers, elected each April, whose job was to arrange care and support for the town's poorest residents.
Help given to people who could not support themselves, such as food, money, or a place to stay. Here, the town organized it through the whole community.

vocabulary.term: in proportion as they are rated
vocabulary.definition: Each family contributed according to how much it was taxed. Wealthier, higher-taxed families were expected to give more.
Assessed for taxes. A family's "rate" was the amount of tax it owed, which the town used to divide the cost of care.

sourcingQuestions: What kind of record is this, and who at the town meeting decided how Johanna Hutton and her child would be cared for?

The town spelled her name "Johanah Hutton" in the minutes but "Joanna Hudson" in the accounts beside the 1782 records. Why should a careful reader treat these as apparently, not certainly, the same person?
This vote was taken in 1783, while Long Island was still occupied. What does it tell you that the town kept handling poor relief during the war?
closeReadingQuestions: The plan says families would keep her "in perposhion as they are Rated." In your own words, how did the town decide which families paid the most?
What can and cannot this short entry tell us about Johanna Hutton herself, such as her age, her health, or how she came to need help?
The clerk's spelling is uneven, with words like "ceape" for keep and "perposhion" for proportion. What does that tell you about writing and record-keeping in this time and place?
historicalThinkingQuestions: Why do historians say Hutton and Hudson are "apparently" the same woman instead of stating it as a fact? What would it take to be sure?
This is one of the few places an ordinary poor woman enters Islip's public record. What do we gain by studying a document like this, and what voices are still missing from it?
The town shared the cost of care across families by tax rate. What does that method suggest about how this community understood its responsibility to its neighbors?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Today, communities still argue about how to share the cost of caring for people in need. How is Islip's 1783 plan, where families gave in proportion to what they were taxed, similar to or different from how we fund help for others now?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: Using only what the record actually says, write a short paragraph describing how Islip planned to care for Johanna Hutton and her child. Then add one sentence beginning "One thing this record does not tell us is..." to name a question the source leaves open.

sourceActivities.title: 1784: Islip Dates Its Records by American Independence
sourceActivities.excerpt: At a town meting of the precenct held the first tuesday in April by the Authority of the good People of the State of new york and in the Eighth year of the American Independence...
sourceActivities.context: For every wartime meeting from 1777 to 1783, Islip's clerk was required to open the town record with the reign of King George III, because that was the standard legal dating formula for official records under Crown authority. It was not a sign that the townspeople were Loyalists. The British left New York in November 1783. When the town gathered the next April, the clerk opened the record with new words: the authority of the people of the State of New York and the year of American independence. Isaac Thompson, who had been supervisor through the whole war, was re-elected under the new heading.

vocabulary.definition: An early unit of local government. Islip was called the Precinct of Islip before it was recognized as a Town.
Counting the year by how long a king has reigned. Wartime clerks were legally required to date records by the reign of King George III; this was a formula, not proof of loyalty to the King.
Counting years from 1776, the year of the Declaration of Independence. The 'Eighth year of the American Independence' means 1784.
The town's top elected officer, who oversaw town business and accounts.

sourcingQuestions: Who wrote this line, and for what kind of record was it written?
Why would a town clerk open a routine meeting record with a statement about who holds authority?
This heading opens the same annual meeting the town had held for decades. What can a record's opening words tell us that the list of officers elected cannot?
closeReadingQuestions: Whose authority does the 1784 heading name, and how is that different from the 'his Majesty King George the third' wording used in the wartime headings?
The clerk writes 'the Eighth year of the American Independence.' Counting forward from 1776, do the numbers add up to 1784? What does that tell you about how the town was now measuring time?
Nothing else about the April 1784 meeting changed: the same offices, the same accounts. If the meeting stayed the same, what exactly changed in this heading?
historicalThinkingQuestions: Historians are careful to say the King's name in the wartime headings was a required legal formula, not proof the townspeople were Loyalists. Why does that distinction matter when we read the 1784 change?
The Revolution is often pictured as battles. Here it arrives as a change in a record's opening words. What does that suggest about how big historical changes can show up in ordinary documents?
Isaac Thompson was re-elected supervisor under both the King's-reign headings and the new independence heading. What does it tell us that the same person led the town through both dating systems?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Governments today still put their authority in official documents, on money, on courthouses, and in oaths. Why does it still matter in whose name a government says it acts?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: Imagine you are Islip's town clerk in April 1784, writing the first meeting record since the British left. In three to four sentences, explain to a reader two hundred years later why you chose to date the record by 'the American Independence' instead of by the King's reign.

sourceActivities.title: Who Lived in Islip: The 1798 Conklin Description

source.label: Nathaniel Conklin's Description of the Town of Islip, 1798 (Primary Sources)

sourceActivities.excerpt: There are in this Town about one hundred and twenty dweling [dwelling] houses twenty-five of which Indians, Mustees and free Negros live in.
sourceActivities.excerptAttribution: Nathaniel Conklin, 'Description of the Town of Islip in Suffolk County,' January 11, 1798
sourceActivities.context: Supervisor Nathaniel Conklin wrote this description in 1798 to defend Islip's boundaries in a dispute with Huntington, but he ended up giving a full portrait of the town fifteen years after the Revolution: the farms, the bay's oysters and clams, the schoolhouses, the taverns, and who lived in the town's roughly 120 houses. In this passage he counts about 25 of those houses as home to Indian, mixed-ancestry, and free Black families. That means roughly one in five households in Islip was Native, mixed-ancestry, or free Black in the first generation after independence.

vocabulary.definition: Houses where people lived. Conklin counted about 120 of them in the whole town.

A term used in the historical period for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African. It is a word from Conklin's time; we explain it as a period term and do not use it as a label for people today.
Conklin's spelling for free Black people, meaning Black residents who were not enslaved. We quote his exact words but describe these neighbors today as free Black residents.
The town's top elected officer. Conklin was Islip's supervisor when he wrote this description.

sourcingQuestions: Conklin wrote this to win a boundary argument with Huntington, not to record the town's people. How might that purpose have shaped what he chose to count and describe?
Conklin was the town supervisor. What did his position let him know about the town's houses and households?
This is one man's description from a single year. What kinds of people living in Islip might a count of 'houses' still leave out or undercount?
closeReadingQuestions: Conklin says about 25 of roughly 120 houses were home to these families. Roughly what fraction of the town's households is that?

The passage groups three different communities, Indian, Mustee, and free Black families, into one sentence. What does that grouping tell us, and what does it hide about these neighbors as distinct people?
Conklin uses the period word 'Mustees' for people of mixed ancestry. Why do we quote his exact word but explain it rather than repeat it as a label for people today?
historicalThinkingQuestions: The Articles of Association that Islip signed in 1775 could not be signed by enslaved people, free Black people, or Native Americans. How does this 1798 count of who actually lived in Islip complicate the story of who the Revolution's promise was for?
This single sentence is one of the only records that names these families' presence at all. What are the risks of reconstructing people's lives from a count made by someone else, for another purpose?
Conklin's description and the town's poor-relief minutes both mention people of color in Islip. Why is it useful for a historian to compare more than one kind of record about the same community?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Islip has been a diverse town since its earliest records, not only in recent times. Why does it matter to know that a community's mix of peoples goes back to its founding generation?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: Conklin's sentence tells us about 25 households existed but almost nothing about the people in them. In four to five sentences, write down three specific questions you would want a historian to research about these Native, mixed-ancestry, and free Black families, and explain why each question matters.

sourceActivities.title: York and Elizabeth: A 1783 Marriage Record
sourceActivities.excerpt: York, a Black servant to William Nicoll, Esq. ... Elizabeth, a free Indian Woman.
sourceActivities.excerptAttribution: St. John's Episcopal Church parish record of a 1783 marriage
sourceActivities.context: A St. John's Episcopal Church parish booklet records a 1783 marriage between York, described as a Black servant to William Nicoll, and Elizabeth, described as a free Indian woman. The church noted that this record, together with others, offers a glimpse of the population of Islip Grange at the time: small, but ethnically mixed. York and Elizabeth appear only briefly in the record, yet they are part of Islip's real history. Records like this one document Black and Native people, some enslaved or formerly enslaved, whose lives were rarely written down.

vocabulary.definition: A record kept by a church of events like baptisms, marriages, and burials. Often it is the only surviving written trace of a person's life.

A word used in the records for a person who worked in a household. In this period the term could describe an enslaved person, so historians read it with care rather than assuming it means paid, freely chosen work.
Not enslaved. The record calls Elizabeth 'a free Indian Woman,' which tells us she was not held in bondage, while York is not described as free.
The name of the early landholding tied to the Nicoll family that became part of the Town of Islip.

sourcingQuestions: This marriage was written down by a church, not by York or Elizabeth themselves. How might that change what the record tells us, and what it leaves out?
The record describes York and Elizabeth by their relationship to William Nicoll and by their status ('servant,' 'free'). What did the person keeping the record think was most important to note?
A church booklet is the only source here. What other kinds of records might a historian look for to learn more about York and Elizabeth's lives?
closeReadingQuestions: The record calls York 'a Black servant to William Nicoll' and Elizabeth 'a free Indian Woman.' What does the difference in how each person is described tell us about their situations?
York's freedom is not mentioned, while Elizabeth's is. What does that silence make you wonder about York's status?
Two people are marrying, yet the record identifies them mostly by race, status, and their tie to a landowner. What does this tell us about how people of color were recorded in this period?
historicalThinkingQuestions: York and Elizabeth appear in just a line or two. Why are brief records like this so important for telling the full history of a community?

We must handle this record with dignity and accuracy. What is the difference between using York and Elizabeth to represent a theme and respecting them as two real people whose inner lives we cannot recover?
Founding stories often center patentees, officials, and landowning families. How does this marriage record change the picture of who lived in early Islip?
sourceActivities.todayQuestion: Every community's history includes people whose names barely made it into the records. Why is it worth the effort today to find, preserve, and honor those brief traces?
sourceActivities.writingPrompt: York and Elizabeth are real people we know almost nothing about. In four to five sentences, write about what this single record does tell us for certain, and then explain why a historian must be careful not to invent details the record does not give.

exhibitGuide.beforeReading: Before you read: When you hear the word "independence," what do you think it promised, and who do you imagine it was meant for? Write down your first guess. You will come back to it at the end.

panels.title: The Promise
panels.question: This exhibit asks what independence meant in 1776, and it also asks who was included in it and who was left outside. As you begin, what does 'the promise of independence' mean to you, and why might one small town be a good place to test that big question?

panels.title: Before the Town
panels.question: Islip has no single founding date: it grew from Native land, to a 1683 land purchase, to a 1710 act that let the precinct elect its own officials. Why do you think this panel says the 1710 act, not the 1683 purchase, is the real beginning of the town's government?

panels.title: Revolution Comes to Islip
panels.question: In May 1775 the people of Islip Precinct signed the Articles of Association even though signing was treason against the Crown, and by 1776 they had formed their own militia company. What does it tell you about the town that Isaac Thompson, a Crown magistrate, signed anyway?

panels.title: Washington's Visit
panels.question: In 1790 Washington stopped for one night at Sagtikos Manor, by then serving as the new nation's first president. The panel is careful to say some popular stories about that visit 'cannot be proven.' Why do you think historians choose careful wording instead of the more exciting version?

panels.title: An Unfinished Promise
panels.question: The Revolution spoke in the language of liberty, yet enslaved and free Black people, Native people, women, children, and Quakers were left out, and enslaved people worked at Sagtikos Manor. How can the promise of independence be both real and unfinished at the same time?

panels.title: Many Roots, One Town
panels.question: From the 1798 households Nathaniel Conklin counted, to the Jewish community whose roots this panel traces through the town's later history, to the Holocaust survivors who found refuge here, the town's story kept widening to include more people. What does this panel show you about how belonging in Islip has changed over time?

panels.title: The Promise Continues
panels.question: This panel says that history is more than the past. It is also what a community chooses to preserve, question, and share. How does scanning and publishing a few handwritten lines from the old town records, like the 1776 gun or the 1784 heading, help keep the promise of independence going today?

exhibitGuide.surprised: What surprised you? Name one thing in these seven panels you did not expect, and explain why it changed how you think about Islip or about American independence.
exhibitGuide.included: Who was included in this story? Using the panels, name the people and groups who could take part in Islip's early government and public life, and describe what gave them that place, such as owning land, holding office, or signing the Articles of Association.
exhibitGuide.leftOut: Who was left out, or is still being researched? Using the panels, name people and groups who were excluded from liberty or whose fuller stories the exhibit says are still part of ongoing 250th research, such as the enslaved and indentured workers at Sagtikos Manor or the communities this exhibit hopes to add as sources become ready.
exhibitGuide.finalReflection: Now return to your first answer. The exhibit calls independence "a beginning, not an ending," and says "the story is not finished." What do you think it means to say the promise of independence is living and unfinished, and what is one way a student, a neighbor, or a whole community could help keep that promise going in Islip today?

timelineActivities.title: Compare the Kids Timeline to the Full Timeline
steps: Pick one event and open it in both places: read the short kids version on the child-friendly timeline (/timeline/kids), then read the full entry on the main timeline (/timeline).
On a piece of paper, write down what the kids version tells you in one or two sentences.
Now read the full entry and list three extra details it adds that the kids version left out (names, dates, numbers, or quoted words).
Underline any place where BOTH versions are careful to say we do not know something for sure. Notice that the simple version stays honest, it does not trade caution for a made-up answer.
Talk it over as a class: what did the writers keep for younger readers, and what did they add for older readers? Why leave some things out?
timelineActivities.example: Use 1776-town-gun. The kids timeline says the town bought a gun and its gear, wrote down that it belonged to the whole town, and the old papers never say why, so it is still a mystery. The full entry adds the clerk's exact words, "A Gun and the accutriments... the property of the towns," and explains that the date is suggestive but the purpose stays an open research question. Then compare 1782-1783-hutton the same way.

timelineActivities.title: Match Each Event to the Source Behind It
steps: Read a small set of timeline entries (/timeline) and notice the "Sources" line at the bottom of each one.
Open the Primary Sources page (/explore/primary-sources) and skim the documents there.
Draw a line from each timeline event to the primary source it comes from. The source is the actual old paper; the timeline entry is the story told from it.
For one pair, copy a few words straight from the source excerpt and show where those words shaped the timeline sentence.
Discuss: could you tell this event's story without the source? What would be missing or uncertain?
timelineActivities.example: Match these real pairs: 1775-articles goes with articles-of-association-1775; 1776-militia-letter goes with thompson-militia-letter-1776; 1765-fishing-rule goes with town-minutes-fishing-1765; 1798-conklin goes with conklin-description-1798; 1777-1783-wartime-meetings goes with town-minutes-wartime.

timelineActivities.title: Put the Events in Order
steps: Read the shuffled list of events below without looking at the dates first, and guess the order from what you already know.
Now open the timeline (/timeline) and find each event to check its real date.
Arrange the cards from earliest to latest, writing the date next to each one.
For each step, write one sentence about how the event before it helped make the next one possible or more likely.
Check your work on the kids timeline (/timeline/kids), which runs in the same order, and fix any cards you had backwards.
timelineActivities.example: Sort this shuffled set into order: 1783-evacuation, 1775-lexington, 1776-occupation-begins, 1775-articles, 1776-battle-long-island, 1784-state-authority. The correct sequence is 1775-lexington (April 19, 1775), 1775-articles (May 10, 1775), 1776-battle-long-island (August 1776), 1776-occupation-begins (September 1776), 1783-evacuation (November 1783), and 1784-state-authority (April 1784).

timelineActivities.title: What Stayed the Same, What Changed
steps: Make two columns on the board: "Stayed the same" and "Changed."
Walk the timeline (/timeline) from the 1770s into the 1780s and find the annual town meeting appearing again and again, year after year.
In the "Stayed the same" column, list the parts that kept going: the April meeting, electing a supervisor, caring for the poor, the rules for hogs on the commons.
In the "Changed" column, list what shifted: who the town said it answered to, and how the clerk dated the records.
Write one sentence explaining why the annual meeting continuing through war and occupation is a strong example of continuity, even while everything around it changed.
Read the kids version of these entries (/timeline/kids) and check that your two columns still hold up in simpler words.
timelineActivities.example: Compare 1775-april-meeting (a normal peacetime meeting), 1777-1783-wartime-meetings (the same meeting held every April under occupation, dated by King George III's regnal year, which was the required legal formula and not proof of Loyalist feeling), and 1784-state-authority (the same meeting, now dated by the year of American independence). The meeting is the continuity; the dating formula is the change.

timelineActivities.title: How Does Evidence Become History?
steps: Start with the raw evidence: open a town-minutes source (/explore/primary-sources) and read the clerk's actual words, kept in their original spelling.
Notice that these were working records, written to run the town, not to tell a story to us. Ask: what were they FOR when they were written?
Find the timeline entry built from that source (/timeline) and see how a historian turned a ledger line into an explained event, adding context and marking what is still uncertain.
Read the same event on the kids timeline (/timeline/kids) to see how it becomes a clear story for young readers while staying honest.

Discuss the chain out loud: clerk writes a record, the record survives and is transcribed and digitized, a historian reads and interprets it, and it becomes a timeline entry teachers can use. Name one place where a careful choice keeps the history honest.
Write a short paragraph answering the question in your own words, using the town minutes as your example.
timelineActivities.example: Work it with the town minutes. The evidence town-minutes-1775-1776 records "A Gun and the accutriments... the property of the towns." The timeline entry 1776-town-gun turns that one line into an explained moment while refusing to claim the gun was for the militia, because the minutes never say. That refusal is exactly how honest history gets made from evidence.
